text: HIGH COURT [190 {HIGH COURT OF AUSTRALIA.) THE COMMISSIONER OF RAILWAYS. . APPELLANT DEFENDANT, AND LEAHY é aa : : ' : . RESPOND: PLAINTIFF. ON APPEAL FROM THE SUPREME COURT OF WESTERN AUSTRALIA.
citation: Commissioner of Railways v Leahy [1904] HCA 30

text: Negligence—Contributory negligence —Functions of judge and jury—Failure to whisll —Duty of defendant where plaintiff guilty of contributory negligence. In an action for negligence, if it appears on the plaintiff's case that he ha been guilty of contributory negligence, the Court should grant a nonsuit 0 direct judgment for the defendant, unless there is also evidence fit for the jury that, notwithstanding the plaintiff's contributory negligence, the defendant, by the exercise of reasonable care, could have averted the injury. In an action of negligence it appeared that the plaintiff was run over by train at a level crossing.
citation: Commissioner of Railways v Leahy [1904] HCA 30

text: She heard a whistle, waited until one trai passed tu her left, crossed the lines immediately behind it, and was knoe down and run over by another train coming in the opposite direction. said that after the first train had passed she looked both ways and saw only the first train. Owing to the peculiar formation of the track she must hay seen the second train if she had actually looked in the direction from which was coming. There was evidence that the second train was a special, and was travelling at an unusually fast rate of speed and did not sound a whit At the trial a verdict was directed for the defendant on the ground of h contributory negligence. 'The Full Court Ae/d that the question of contributory negligence could not be withdrawn from the jury, and that the judgment must be set aside.
citation: Commissioner of Railways v Leahy [1904] HCA 30

text: Held, (Grigith C.J. dubitante,) that the nonsuit must be set aside on ground that there was sufficient evidence to go to the jury that the defendant servants, by the exercise of ordinary care, could have averted the accident. Decision of the Full Court of Western Australia affirmed. Coyle v.
citation: Commissioner of Railways v Leahy [1904] HCA 30

text: Great Northern Railway Co. of Ireland, 20 L.R., Ir., 409, follow Apres from an order of the Supreme Court of Western Australi (4th October, 1904), 2 OLR] OF AUSTRALIA. 55 An action had been brought by the respondent against H.C. or A the appellant for damages for injuries sustained by her under 1%* the circumstances set forth in the judgment of Griffith C.J. ee The action was tried before Mr. Commissioner Roe and a jury, Oey or and by direction a verdict was returned for the defendant, ae and judgment entered accordingly. On appeal, the Full Court , of Western Australia ordered the judgment to be set aside and a new trial to be had between the parties on the grounds that— (1) There was evidence in support of the plaintiff's claim proper to be left to the jury, and the learned Commissioner was wrong in withdrawing the case from the jury and directing judgment for the defendant; and (2) the learned Commissioner was wrong in refusing to leave to the jury the questions :—(a) that if there was any contributory negligence on the part of the plaintiff the defendant could, nevertheless, by the exercise of ordinary care and precaution have avoided the injury complained of; (b) whether or not the plaintiff was put off her guard by reason of the negligence of the defendant or his servants, and whether or not, under the peculiar circumstances of the case, she might reasonably be excused in acting as she did, or alternatively under the said peculiar circumstances whether or not there was anything to create a sense of danger.
citation: Commissioner of Railways v Leahy [1904] HCA 30

text: The facts are sufficiently stated in the judgment of the Chief Justice. Barker for the appellant. It was admitted that there was evidence of negligence in the defendant, but not such negligence as to cause the accident.
citation: Commissioner of Railways v Leahy [1904] HCA 30

text: The plaintiff, if she did not actually cause the accident, at any rate so contributed to it as to be guilty of contributory negligence. She must either have looked and seen the train, or not looked at all. There is no evidence connecting the alleged negligence of the defendant or the absence of whistling with the happening of the accident.
citation: Commissioner of Railways v Leahy [1904] HCA 30

text: Taking the case most favourable to the plaintiff, and supposing she neglected to look, she was still guilty of contributory negligence. Nothing could be more negligent than to cross without looking when she sees in front of her the rails and a warning notice. There are not two inferences open here on which the case could be sent to the jury.
citation: Commissioner of Railways v Leahy [1904] HCA 30

text: London und South-Western Railway Co. (8). The principle of all the cases is, that, if the plaintiff shows contributory negligence on his part, and gives an excuse for his negligence, it is a question for a jury, and not for a Judge, to say whether that excuse is sufficient. In Slattery's case, it was known that the train was coming.

text: It was the duty of the Judge to sen the case to a jury to determine whose negligence caused th accident: Tug? v. Warman (1). It is not necessary for the plaintiff to show that the accident happened solely through the defendant's negligence: Radley v.

As to the contention of the plaintiff that, notwithstanding 19 'contributory negligence, if it could be shown that the defendant, px Comms. by the exercise of ordinary care, could have averted the accident, PfoNw Or . the onus is on the plaintiff, and, before it can be left to the jury, fe there must be evidence to support it.

text: Here there is none. The expert evidence on this is mere conjecture and should not be left to the jury. The mere failure to perform a self-imposed duty is not actionable negligence : Skelton v.

text: London and North-Western Railway Co. (3). * GrirritH C.J. This isan appeal from the decision of theSupreme Oetober 17. Court of Western Australia setting aside a judgment for the defendant in an action heard before Mr.
citation: Commissioner of Railways v Leahy [1904] HCA 30

text: Commissioner Roe and a " jury at Kalgoorlie, in which the learned Commissioner directed the jury to return a verdict for the defendant. The action was brought by the plaintiff against the Commissioner of Railways for damages for injuries sustained by her by reason of the alleged negligence of the defendant's servants. In the first instance it was set up on behalf of the defendant that the plaintiff was guilty of negligence which contributed to the injury, and at the trial an _ amendment was made in the pleadings setting out that, if the plaintiff was guilty of that negligence, the defendant could notwithstanding, by the exercise of reasonable care, have avoided _ the injuries complained of.
citation: Commissioner of Railways v Leahy [1904] HCA 30

text: The facts are in an extremely small compass. They give rise to a question of law which is in one sense of considerable difficulty, although the difficulty is not so much as to principles of law as of the application of the settled principles to the facts. The plaintiff was run down by a train at a level crossing near Kalgoorlie. The locus in quo was situated at the middle of the are of a somewhat sharp curve, at a point where there is a double line of rails which runs from Kalgoorlie to Boulder. Approaching the crossing from the convex side Kalgoorlie is to the right; trains therefore coming from Kalgoorlie where they pass the crossing go on a curve receding to the left hand, trains coming from Boulder go on a curve receding to the (1) LR. 7 HLL,, 213, at p. 233. (2) 1 T.L.R., 406. (3) L.R., 2C.P., 631.
citation: Commissioner of Railways v Leahy [1904] HCA 30

text: HIGH COURT right hand. At that place the line is perfectly free and 9] and is fenced up to the level crossing, where there are eal pits to prevent cattle straying on to the line. The plai was coming down the road towards the level crossing fi the convex side of the curve about ten o'clock in the mornit 'As she approached the railway she heard the whistle of a tra to her right, coming from Kalgoorlie.
citation: Commissioner of Railways v Leahy [1904] HCA 30

text: She waited and sa train pass the level crossing, passing to her left. When the tra had passed she approached close to the rails, and she said # when she was near the crossing she looked up the line to the I and right and saw no other train. Now, it appears from wl happened afterwards that there was another train to her le coming from Boulder, and it must at that moment have be hidden from her view by the passing train from Kalgoorlie towards Boulder.
citation: Commissioner of Railways v Leahy [1904] HCA 30

text: The curve of the line would cause that to obstruct her view of the line for a considerable distance. three hundred yards from where she must have been sta at the crossing. As soon as the train had passed she started go across the line. She says that, as soon as she got into the foot way, the space between the two lines of railway, she l both to the right and to the left and saw no train, and hea whistle. At that point, however, she had a clear view to the for at least three hundred yards.
citation: Commissioner of Railways v Leahy [1904] HCA 30

text: She walked on, and havit walked about six feet, or at most*nine feet, she was st the engine of the train coming from Boulder towards Kalgoo Now, from the fact that she says, and insists, that she saw no tra whereas it is undoubted that a train was there, and that was a clear view for three hundred yards, it is obvious that either saw the train and walked into it, which is an absurd pi position, or that she did not look. I think it is obvious that § did not look. So that, on the one hand we have evidence to to the jury that the defendant's train, which was a special ti did not whistle, which is some evidence of negligence, and on other the fact established upon her own evidence, although 1 admitted by her, that she did not look and see the train upon her from the left. There can be no doubt that, in a crossing like that, in broad daylight, it is the duty of everyone look and see if a train is coming.
citation: Commissioner of Railways v Leahy [1904] HCA 30

text: That would be so, even 20LR.] OF AUSTRALIA. 61 there was no notice; but it appears that there is a notice at H. C. or A. this particular spot, warning people to "Look out for the train." ke So we have these two facts, on the one hand evidence of negpyre Coss. ligence on the part of the defendant, on the other hand evidence pa that the plaintiffdid not look. It is true she said she looked, v% and no doubt she did look while the view was obstructed by the pasate other train; but the obligation to look, involves, to my mind, the Sr"? °7obligation to look at a time when you can see.
citation: Commissioner of Railways v Leahy [1904] HCA 30

text: Seeing that there was negligence on the part of the defendant in not whistling, and also on the part of the plaintiff in not looking, what then is to be the consequence? The learned Commissioner was asked to direct judgment for the defendant, and he did so, and gave his reasons, holding that contributory negligence had been proved in the plaintift's case, and that, therefore, he was justified in withdrawing the case from the jury. On the case going to the Supreme Court the learned Judges appear to have been of the opinion that, in a case where contributory negligence is set up, the case cannot be withdrawn from the jury.
citation: Commissioner of Railways v Leahy [1904] HCA 30

text: A great number of authorities were referred to in the very able argument which has been addressed to us; but the only opinion I have been able to find to the effect that contributory negligence cannot be withdrawn from the jury, is the opinion of Lord Penzance in Slattery's Case (1). I will mention one or two cases which have been referred to. One is the case of Brown vy.
citation: Commissioner of Railways v Leahy [1904] HCA 30

text: The Great Western Railway (2). In that case Lord Justice Bowen said: "Whenever facts which are not in conflict admit of two reasonable constructions, one in favour of the plaintiff, the other in favour of the defendant, it is for the jury and not for the Judge to draw the inference. On the other hand, where the facts admit of but one reasonable construction, it is for the Judge to decide the case upon the only ground on which it can be decided by any reasonable man.
citation: Commissioner of Railways v Leahy [1904] HCA 30

text: To apply that principle, in Davey v. London and South-Western Railway Co. the facts showed only one possible view—viz., that the plaintiff himself was responsible for the accident, and therefore the Judge was bound to non-suit. So in Wakelin y.
citation: Commissioner of Railways v Leahy [1904] HCA 30

text: London and South-Western Railway the facts were not in dispute, and the only possible view was that noone could tell how the accident happened. The same (1) 3 App. Cas., 1155, at p. 1175. (2) 1 T.L.R., 614. 62 HIGH COURT H.C. oF A. principle is to be applied in this case.
citation: Commissioner of Railways v Leahy [1904] HCA 30

text: It is clear that two m as bic able views may be taken, or, at all events, that there is a r ars Coane able view consistent with the plaintift's right to recover. The HONE OF the learned Judge could not draw his own inference of fact." = that, if there are two reasonable views to be taken on the evide Lrany. d 4 Z or if there is any inference to be drawn from the evidence, the & Grint is one for the jury; but if not, it is for the Judge to withdn the case from the jury. The whole matter was very fully d cussed in the judgment of Chief Baron Palles in the case Coyle v.
citation: Commissioner of Railways v Leahy [1904] HCA 30

text: Great Northern Railway Co. of Ireland (1). The learn Judge, after reviewing the whole of the cases on the subj said (p. 418): "I have anxiously considered the cases cited, ai many others, with a view to form an opinion satisfactory my own mind upon the subject; and I venture to think it be found that the following proposition is correct in point of la and consistent with, if not established by, all the authorities that, to justify the Judge in leaving the case to the jury, withstanding the voluntary act of the injured person, whi contributed to the injury complained of, the circumstances mu: be such as either, firstly, to make the question whether that: is negligent (either per se, or having regard to the conduct of defendants inducing or affecting it), a question of fact; secondly, the circumstances must be such as to render reasonal an inference of fact, that the defendants, by using due care, coul have obviated the consequences of the plaintiff's negligence. — the case be so clear that the determination of those two question involves no inference of fact, it is for the Judge and not for t jury." Laccept that as laying down the law correctly on th subject. I have already pointed out that in this case the con of the plaintiff in not looking is primé facie negligence. says she looked, therefore she was not put off from lookit by the omission of the defendant's engine driver to whistle. will proceed to apply these two tests, and first inquire wheth . her act in not looking was negligent either per se or havin regard to the conduct of the defendant inducing or affectir it.
citation: Commissioner of Railways v Leahy [1904] HCA 30

text: There is no evidence that her negligence was induced (1) 20L.R., Ir, 409, 2C.LR.] OF AUSTRALIA, affected by any act of the defendant, so that, if that was all there is in the case, the duty of the learned Commissioner was either to grant a non-suit or to enter judgment for the defendant, as the case might be. The other question is whether, the plaintiff having been guilty of the negligence of not looking, and of walking in front of the train, the driver could, by the exercise of reasonable care, have obviated the effect of her negligence. Tt was suggested that this was possible in two ways: He might have pulled up his train before he got to her.
citation: Commissioner of Railways v Leahy [1904] HCA 30

text: The facts as to that are that, after she reached the place where she could have seen the train if she had looked, she had walked seven, eight, or, at most, nine feet, before she was struck by the train. On the other hand, the driver of the approaching train might have seen her while she was walking part of that distance. As to whether the engine driver could have pulled up in the time, that seems to me to be a difficult question.
citation: Commissioner of Railways v Leahy [1904] HCA 30

text: The suggestion is that he ought to have whistled, and, that if he had done so, it would have avoided the accident. Mr. Barker contends that the onus of proof of that is on the plaintiff, and there is no doubt that that is so.
citation: Commissioner of Railways v Leahy [1904] HCA 30

text: The question is whether, the facts being as I have stated, and the precise distance not being exactly known, and it being a matter of a few seconds only, there was evidence to leave to the jury from which they would have been justified in finding that the defendant could have avoided the accident by the exercise of reasonable care. I confess I have very great difficulty in coming to the conclusion that there was enough evidence to leave to the jury on that point ; but as my learned brothers think that there was, I am not prepared to dissent from them. On the other points I agree with them.
citation: Commissioner of Railways v Leahy [1904] HCA 30

text: Barton J. In this case the plaintiff, in her statement of claim, alleges that when she was injured she was walking across the railway line between Kalgoorlie and Boulder, at a level crossing known as the Broad Arrow Crossing, where the defendant's railway line intersected a public thoroughfare. Of this there is of course no question.
citation: Commissioner of Railways v Leahy [1904] HCA 30

text: It is also alleged that the defendant had not erected or had not kept up any fences, gates, or other protection to his said railway line at the place. 'That contention was H. C. or A. 1904. == Tue ComisSIONER OF Ratiways v. Leany.
citation: Commissioner of Railways v Leahy [1904] HCA 30

text: Griffith O.J. 64 HIGH COURT H.C. or A. dropped. Another paragraph says that "It was the duty of 1904. defendant's engine driver, or other servant to blow a whistle, eee do some other act, to notify or to warn persons lawfully crossi sone oF the said railway line at the said place of the approach of ane v on his said line. Alternatively, it was the duty of the defend, take reasonable precautions to make the said crossing reasonab Barton J. safe for persons using the same." And then she says that, " the 9th of August, 1903, and in breach of his said duties, an engin coming from Boulder, rushed at a fast pace over the crossing, n whistle having been blown, or other warning given." And, t last paragraph states that "by reason of the matters aforesaid t plaintiff was struck and run over by the engine, beth her le being so injured that they were subsequently amputated, and h body severely bruised internally.
citation: Commissioner of Railways v Leahy [1904] HCA 30

text: Against that statement of a defence is entered in which all material facts are denied, an then the defendant alleges that the train by which the plaint was injured on the occasion alleged approached and passed o the crossing at a moderate and reasonable pace, and after whistle on the engine had been blown twice." Further, #l "the plaintiff's injuries were not caused as alleged, but by th plaintiff's recklessly, and negligently, and without keeping | proper look out while she was crossing the railway line, ste that on which the train by which the plaintiff was kno down was moving." And that, "in any event, if the defenda or his servants were guilty of any breach of duty or neglig (which is denied), the plaintiff's conduct contributed to b about her injuries, and the injuries and accident would not happened but for her negligence in stepping in front of a approaching train in the mamner alleged." Negligence conne with the accident on the part of the defendant appears to b admitted, I mean admitted for the purpose of this argument The gravamen of the defendant's contention is the contributor negligence of the plaintiff. On the statement of defence plaintiff joined issue on the question of contributory negli; and she was afterwards allowed to make a further allegation by way of amendment, that, if there was any contributory negligence 2 OLR.) OF AUSTRALIA. the defendant could notwithstanding by the exercise of ordinary eare have avoided the accident. Now, it appears that the plaintiff, was, with some friends about to cross the line at the Broad Arrow Road crossing.
citation: Commissioner of Railways v Leahy [1904] HCA 30

text: Her friends went on and crossed the line, ' but she, remembering that she had left something behind her, went back for it and returned after her friends. She says in her evidence, "I saw my companions had crossed the line, as I got to the line—I heard a train whistle, coming from Kalgoorlie to Boulder —I waited and looked both ways as the train was coming up to me—I could see nothing else—I waited until the tail of the passenger train had cleared the signal post—I looked after it, and looked again both ways before I proceeded to cross—I heard nothing—I had crossed there many times—There was no whistle after the passenger train had passed—If a train had whistled I would have heard it—I crossed the passenger train lines and had got between the other two rails when the accident oceurred—Up to that moment I had not seen or heard the other train": that is, a special train coming in the opposite direction, from Boulder to Kalgoorlie. In cross-examination the plaintiff said : "You have an uninterrupted view up to Hannan's Street after you cross the first line, there was nothing to obstruct my view—I looked, and did not see any train." There are other witnesses to corroborate the plaintiff, and what appears is this, taking the evidence altogether, the passing of the passenger train and the coming up of the special train were within a very short space of time. She said in another portion of her evidence that she had always heard trains whistle approaching that-crossing, and that the passenger train, which was approaching the crossing, whistled either just before or just after she saw it.
citation: Commissioner of Railways v Leahy [1904] HCA 30

text: It does not appear to be very material which, because it was the whistling which apparently caused her to pause. That train passed, and it is true, I think, that at the time that train passed, as she looked down Hannan Street, her view of Hannan Street Station and of the train approaching thence, 7.e., from Boulder towards Kalgoorlie, would be to some extent obstructed by the receding train. Upon the evidence we have to consider, the engine driver of the special train, who, according to regulations, should have whistled three hundred yards from the crossing, did not whistle at all.
citation: Commissioner of Railways v Leahy [1904] HCA 30

text: H.C. oF A. relation to the whistling may appear if the case goes to anotl Tue CommisSIONER OF Raitways LS 1904. at Barton J, HIGH COURT {19 trial : but taking the case as it stands there was no whistle at thy hundred yards or at all. From that point on the case is not extremely clear one; but this seems clear, that a few momen before, the plaintiff had been warned by a whistle, she h taken that warning, and had acted with the ordinary degree ¢ prudence which would be expected from a reasonable perso Afterwards she went upon the line and the engine of a 5 train, which had not whistled, struck her. It has been conte at the bar that it was not possible in the time left to him the engine-driver to have pulled up his train. He was first all in fault for not whistling, and in the next place, I amn clear that he could not have pulled up his train, and I am b means clear that he could not have so decreased the speed o} train as to have given the plaintiff a chance to cross the Ordinarily speaking, the action of a reasonable person wot have been to step back immediately the warning of the appro of a train was given, as the plaintiff stepped back on the approt of the first train. In the case of the second train the opportuni to step back was not given, though I admit that there does se to be evidence of contributory negligence on the part of t plaintiff, who might, if she had looked more carefully, haye e the approach of the train. text: Great Northern Railway Co. of Ireland (1): " Whether bass, it was a causa causans depends on this—Was there a neglect on Tax Commsthe part of the defendants of anything, whereby the consequences O05¥, 0. of the plaintiff's negligence might have been prevented." And ae then again (2)—* To justify the Judge in leaving the case : to the jury, notwithstanding the voluntary act of the injured person, which contributed to the injury complained of, the cireumstances must be such as either, firstly, to make the question whether that act is negligent (either per se, or having regard to the conduct of the defendants inducing or affecting it), a question of fact ; or secondly, the circumstances must be such as to render reasonable an inference of fact, that the defendants, by using due care, could have obviated the consequences of the plaintiff's negligence. Ifthe case be so clear that the determination of these two questions involves no inference of fact, it is for the Judge, and not for the jury." Now, was the question, whether the plaintiff's act in going on was negligent, either by itself, or haying regard to the conduct of the defendant's inducing or affecting it, a question of fact? I think it was.
citation: Commissioner of Railways v Leahy [1904] HCA 30

text: I think, on the whole it was a question for a jury to weigh the respective facts and inferences, so as to say which was the causa causans, the act of the plaintiff or the act of the defendant, whether the contributory negligence of the plaintiff, of which there js some evidence, was such that, notwithstanding any conduct of the defendant, the accident would have happened, or whether on the other hand, the accident would have happened notwithstanding any conduct of the plaintiff. I find that a difficult question to decide, and I think I ought to be clear that, the plaintiff's conduct manifestly was the cwusa causans, before I say that the learned Commissioner was right in withdrawing the case from the jury. So there are, at least, two questions to be determined, and one must be clear upon the determination of them that there is no evidence of fact for the jury.

The difficulty of dealing with question of onus in cases like the present arises from the fact tha' in most cases it is well nigh impossible for the plaintiff to lay h evidence before a jury or the Court without disclosing ci stances which either point to or tend to rebut the conclusion th the injured party was guilty of contributory negligence.

text: Slattery: 'If such contributory negligence be admitted by the plaintiff, or be proved by the plaintiff's witnesses while establishing negligence against the defendants, I do not think there is anything left for the jury to decide, there being no contest of fact." The contributory negligence there dealt with is negligence on the part of the plaintiff without which, on a review of the whole facts, the injury would not have taken place. Following the reasons of Lord Watson it cannot be doubted that he aptly concludes with that quotation from Lord Hatherly, and for myself I think that presents a reasonable and sutisfactory explanation. Was contributory negligence admitted by the plaintiff or proved by the plaintiff's witnesses?
citation: Commissioner of Railways v Leahy [1904] HCA 30

text: If so, and if it was a class of contributory negligence which deprives the plaintiff of her right of action, nobody can doubt that there was nothing left for the jury to decide, and that the Commissioner would have been right in directing a verdict for the defendant. In Radley's Case (1), Lord Penzance puts the matter this way (2): "The remaining question is, whether the learned Judge properly directed the jury in point of law. The law in these cases of negligence is perfectly settled, and is beyond dispute.
citation: Commissioner of Railways v Leahy [1904] HCA 30

text: The first proposition is a general one to this effect— that the plaintiff in an action for negligence cannot succeed if it is found by the jury that he has himself been guilty of any negligence or want of ordinary care which contributed to the cause of the accident. But there is another proposition equally well established, and it is a qualification upon the first, namely, that although the plaintiff might have been guilty of negligence, and although that negligence might have in fact contributed to the accident, yet if the defendant could in the result, by the exercise of ordinary care and diligence have avoided the mischief which happened, the plaintift's negligence would not excuse him." (1 46 LJ. Ex., 573. (2) 46 L.J., Ex., at p.
citation: Commissioner of Railways v Leahy [1904] HCA 30

text: HIGH COURT [190 Reviewing then together the passages I have read, which ar perfectly consistent with each other, the question here is this Could the defendant, notwithstanding the negligence of th plaintiff contributing to the accident, by the exercise of ordinary care and diligence have avoided the mischief which happened If so, the plaintiff's negligence will not excuse the defendant, shall exercise a wise discretion by not making any reference to the facts which might be unduly used at that second trial, I fe myself compelled to go no further in applying the law to facts than I have done. If a jury came to the conclusion on these facts that by precautions, not extraordinary, but ordina well-known, and applied, not necessarily easily, it may be wi this train from knocking down and inflicting injury upon plaintiff, and if they come to the conclusion that it would hay been possible for the defendant to have prevented the accident notwithstanding the plaintiff's negligence, then the question should have put to myself would be: Can I say that such verdict was one which any reasonable man could have properly given? Well, I do not feel myself prepared to answer that question by saying that such a verdict would be in that sense ont which no reasonable man could have found. I think that in thi case, coming before us at such a stage, I am bound to express the opinion that the jury should have had an opportunity of finding upon these facts. That is all I do say, and for that reason I thinl the decision of the Commissioner should be set aside and that th ease should go down for a new trial.

text: I think it would be useful if I quote the remarks of the Master of the Rolls, afterwards Lord Esher, who says (2): "When ought a Judge to take a case out of the hands of a jury? When is he entitled todo so? Let us see what Mr.

text: Justice Field says :—'1 say I may take it into my own hands when no reasonable jury, acting fairly and impartially between the plaintiff and the defendant, ought to draw or would draw any but one conclusion, and that conclusion is conclusive against the plaintiff; then I must take the case into my own hands.'" In applying that principle, a Judge cannot take the decision of the facts into his own hands, and refuse to allow the jury to decide upon them, unless in regard to those facts there is only one conclusion which reasonable men can draw, and that is a conclusion against the plaintiff. The whole of the main case really depends upon the defence ot contributory negiigence. As I take it there is, at all events, evidence to go to the jury that, considering the situation of this level crossing, there was negligence on the part of the defendant in failing to whistle on approaching the level crossing. As has already been observed by my learned brothers, we are dealing now only with the case as it was proved for the plaintiff.

Now, the pux Comms: whole of the evidence as to that part of the case really amounts to this—the plaintiff says, and some of her witnesses support her her in this—that she did not move from the position which she occupied while the passenger train was passing until after it had passed the signal post which is just twenty yards away from the crossing, and at that time she was some eight or nine feet from the line which the passenger train had crossed—she described the crossing as the length of the room, away from her, but we are told that it was something like eight or nine feet.

text: She said when the passenger train had got twenty yards away from the crossing, she walked across the line; so that she had to move from where she was standing, to cross the first line, and to cross the six feet way before she got on to the line on which there was danger. Now, according to the evidence of the plaintiff, it seems to be clear enough that the defendant's engine driver could, if he had been on the look-out, have seen the plaintiff, at all events, from the time when she cleared the passenger train; and as the passenger train went on towards Hannan Street, it was opening up more and more the view for the driver of the special. I think we may fairly infer that, at all events, he could have seen the plaintiff after she had crossed the line of rails on which the passenger train was running, and when she got to the six feet way. It is alleged that, when he saw her there, it was his duty to whistle.

The question is whether on those facts there is sufficient evidence to go to the jury of negligence by the defendant of some precaution which a reasonable man under the circumstances would have taken to avert the consequences of the plaintiff's negligence.
